did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a parallel Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model with transfer learning and data augmentation to classify nine types of skin cancer (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) from skin lesion images. The study reports performance metrics such as 79.45% accuracy, 0.76 weighted average precision, and 0.78 weighted average recall for this AI-based classification task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a parallel Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods including data preprocessing, augmentation, hyperparameter tuning, and training on a dataset of 9 skin cancer types. The study reports original results, including performance metrics (79.45% accuracy, 0.76 weighted average precision, 0.78 weighted average recall) and compares them against VGG-16 and VGG-19 models. Although published in a conference proceedings (WIECON-ECE), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Detection and Classification of Skin Cancer by Using a Parallel CNN Model
journal: 2020 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated parallel Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model to classify nine types of skin cancer, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, from skin lesion images. The dataset comprises nine clinical types of skin cancer, such as actinic keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesions, which were processed using image augmentation techniques like rotation and zooming to enrich the data. The model utilizes transfer learning to improve classification accuracy without relying on pre-trained weights from ImageNet. Performance was evaluated using weighted average precision, recall, F1-score, and overall accuracy metrics. The proposed method achieved a 79.45% accuracy, 0.76 weighted average precision, 0.78 weighted average recall, and 0.76 weighted average F1-score. These results indicate that the parallel CNN approach effectively distinguishes between the nine skin cancer classes, offering a potential tool for early detection and diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Nine discrete clinical types of skin cancer (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma, vascular lesions).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to classify skin cancer into nine classes using a parallel CNN model. It reports classification metrics including accuracy (79.45%), weighted average precision (0.76), weighted average recall (0.78), and weighted average F1-score (0.76). The model uses a softmax activation function for classifying images into these discrete categories.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of clinical skin cancer images. The paper states the dataset is categorized according to descriptions taken from ISIC and is public with metadata available for comparison. It contains nine clinical types of skin cancer.

use.training: Used for training the parallel CNN model with data augmentation (rotation, shear, zoom, horizontal flip).
use.validation: Used for validation of the model.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's classification performance.
use.other: The dataset is described as public, allowing for comparisons to each image via metadata.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and compared qualitatively against VGG-16 and VGG-19 models without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset (Kaggle).notes: The proposed model outperformed VGG-16 and VGG-19 in all reported metrics. The dataset consisted of 25,780 images categorized into 9 classes. No pre-trained weights (e.g., ImageNet) were used for the proposed model.

architecture_used.pretraining: No pre-trained weights (e.g., ImageNet) were used; transfer learning approach mentioned but explicitly stated as not using pre-trained weights for the proposed model.

architecture_details.feature_extraction_phase: Five dilated convolutionary units (CBr) with filter sizes n = 64, 128, 256, 512, 512. Input provides two parallel CBr=iConv blocks. Dilation rates (di) vary (1, 2, 3, ..., N). Alternating max-pooling layers. Class activation maps generated coupled to the last convolution layer.
architecture_details.classification_phase: Two-layer Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) / Fully Connected (FC) layers. Each FC layer followed by a dropout layer. FC layer sizes: 4096 and 4096. Dropout rate: 0.5.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation including rotation (range 90), shear (range 0.1), zoom (range 0.14), and horizontal flip
conclusions: The proposed parallel Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model achieved a classification accuracy of 79.45%, which outperformed the VGG-16 (69.57%) and VGG-19 (71.19%) models on the ISIC dataset.
The model demonstrated strong performance with a weighted average precision of 0.76, weighted average recall of 0.78, and weighted average F1-score of 0.76.
The parallel CNN architecture, utilizing dilated convolutional units and transfer learning without pre-trained ImageNet weights, effectively distinguishes between nine clinical types of skin cancer.
Data augmentation techniques, including rotation, shear, zoom, and horizontal flip, were effective in enriching the dataset and mitigating overfitting during training.
The study acknowledges that further research is needed to analyze and accomplish a diversified data collection of substantial amounts of evidence regarding skin cancer to improve the model.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC dataset without testing on independent clinical cohorts or diverse skin tones.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the performance differences between the proposed model and VGG-16/VGG-19.
The model was trained without pre-trained ImageNet weights, which may limit feature extraction capabilities compared to standard transfer learning approaches.
The paper acknowledges the need for diversified data collection to improve model stability and validation, indicating potential bias in the current dataset.
The dataset size (25,503 images) is relatively small for deep learning, necessitating extensive data augmentation (rotation, shear, zoom, flip) to prevent overfitting.
The study classifies nine skin cancer types, including benign lesions, which increases the complexity and potential for misclassification compared to binary melanoma detection tasks.
The paper is a conference proceeding with limited scope, and the authors suggest future work to integrate more sophisticated methodologies like Belief Rule Based Expert Systems.
